input.counterSource: CSS-Tricks "Masonry Layout is Now grid-lanes" notes that item-flow/gap-filling behavior details (item-pack: dense || collapse) are "still in the works" in the spec, suggesting the exact algorithm is not yet fully standardized — but this qualifies future behavior, not the described stacking-axis principle.
input.evidence: The supporting quote is confirmed verbatim on the MDN masonry layout page (last updated March 9, 2026): "On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps." The claim is a near-exact paraphrase of this text and does not overreach it. MDN is a primary, authoritative CSS reference. No credible source contradicts the described behavior — all secondary sources (CSS-Tricks, DEV Community, OpenReplay) corroborate that masonry layout fills gaps rather than leaving them. The specification has been renamed from `masonry` to `grid-lanes` in 2026, but the stacking-axis gap-filling behavior is described consistently across sources. The "rows rise up" phrasing is mechanistically loose (the algorithm is actually column-packing, placing each item in the shortest column), but this imprecision originates in MDN's own text, not in the claim itself — the claim does not introduce a new error. Browser support remains experimental/flagged as of 2026, but this is a support caveat, not a refutation of the behavioral description.
